verses.commentary: Matthew opens with biblos geneseos (βίβλος γενέσεως, book of genesis), echoing Genesis 2:4 and 5:1. Two covenant lines converge in Jesus: David (Davidic king, 2 Samuel 7) and Abraham (Abrahamic blessing to nations, Genesis 12:3). The Messiah fulfills both promises in one person.

The patriarchal line. Election narrows: not Ishmael but Isaac, not Esau but Jacob, not the firstborn but Judah (Genesis 49:10 — scepter not departing from Judah). God works through chosen seed, not natural succession.

First woman named: Tamar (Genesis 38), a Canaanite involved in sexual scandal who tricked Judah into giving her his seed. Matthew flags grace through unlikely women, anticipating Mary. The line is preserved through scandal, not despite it.
Nahshon: tribal leader of Judah in the wilderness (Numbers 1:7, 7:12). The royal line passes through exodus leadership. Genealogy carries hidden narrative.

Two more Gentile women: Rahab the Canaanite prostitute of Jericho (Joshua 2) and Ruth the Moabite (whose people were excluded from Israel — Deuteronomy 23:3). The Messiah's bloodline runs through outsiders welcomed by faith.
Bathsheba unnamed, called only the wife of Uriah — the scandal preserved in the indictment. Even David's adultery and murder are woven into messianic ancestry. No sanitizing.
The royal succession through Solomon. The kingdom established and corrupted in the same generation. Matthew tracks the line through the throne of Judah.

Three kings omitted between Jehoram and Uzziah (Ahaziah, Joash, Amaziah of 1 Chronicles 3:11-12). Matthew compresses to fit the fourteen-generation scheme. Theological structure over biological completeness.
The Davidic line continues through faithful Uzziah and unfaithful Ahaz. Genealogy does not screen for righteousness — the promise endures through corrupt kings.
Hezekiah (reformer), Manasseh (worst idolater, 2 Kings 21), Amon, Josiah (reformer). The line oscillates between revival and apostasy. God preserves the promise through judgment.
The exile to Babylon: second great pivot in the structure. The royal line collapses, the throne lost. Yet the genealogy continues — God's promise survives political catastrophe.
Post-exilic resumption. Zerubbabel rebuilds the temple under Persian rule (Ezra 3) but no king is restored. The Davidic throne lies dormant — awaiting fulfillment.
Names unknown to the Old Testament — the silent centuries. Genealogy preserved by family memory through Greek and Roman occupation.
The line presses on through obscurity. No prophets, no kings, no narrative — only continuity. God prepares the Messiah in hidden generations.
Final generations before Joseph. The royal line has shrunk to a Galilean carpenter's household. Greatness will return through humiliation.

The genealogy breaks its pattern. Not 'Joseph the father of Jesus' but 'Joseph the husband of Mary' — and Mary the one of whom (ex hes, ἐξ ἧς, feminine relative pronoun) Jesus was born. Matthew flags the virgin birth before announcing it.

Three sets of fourteen generations. Fourteen = 2 × 7 (double perfection), or gematria of DVD (David, ד+ו+ד = 4+6+4 = 14). Structured theology: Abraham to David (promise made), David to exile (promise lost), exile to Messiah (promise fulfilled).

Genesis (γένεσις, origin) used a second time — new creation language. Betrothal (mnesteuo, μνηστεύω) in first-century Judaism was legally binding; only divorce could dissolve it. The pregnancy 'is from' (ek, ἐκ) the Holy Spirit — divine origin stated plainly.

Joseph is dikaios (δίκαιος, righteous). Here righteousness expresses mercy within Torah — public exposure under Deuteronomy 22:23-24 would shame Mary, possibly fatally. He chooses quiet divorce: legal protection without humiliation.

Angel addresses Joseph as son of David — his royal lineage carries weight even in a dream. Joseph echoes the Genesis Joseph: dreamer, descender into Egypt, savior of his family. The conception is by Holy Spirit, not human will.
Jesus = Yeshua (יְשׁוּעַ, YHWH saves). The name interprets the mission: not Roman expulsion but sin removal. He will save 'his people' — Israel first, but the genealogy already hinted at wider scope.
First of Matthew's ten fulfillment formulas (hina plerothe, ἵνα πληρωθῇ). The OT is the script; Jesus is the protagonist. Prophecy and event interpret each other.

Isaiah 7:14 quoted. Parthenos (παρθένος, virgin) translates Hebrew almah (young woman). Immanuel = ʿImmanu El (עִמָּנוּ אֵל, God with us). The incarnation summarized in one Hebrew name. Bookend to Matthew 28:20 ('I am with you always').
Obedience without recorded speech. Joseph speaks no word in Matthew's entire Gospel; he only acts. Faith demonstrated by motion, not declaration.

Virginity until the birth preserved. Joseph names the child — legally adopting him into the Davidic line. The Messiah enters David's house through adoption, not biological descent. Naming is paternal claim.
